food_subgroup: Stir-fried noodle dish with seafood and egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific concept for Char Kway Teow. Nearest parent is a stir-fried noodle dish concept. A new concept proposal covering this Singapore-Chinese hawker staple is warranted.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite noodle dishes — stir-fried mixed noodle dish (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A03SM used as closest parent for composite stir-fried noodle dishes; no exact code exists for Char Kway Teow.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Char Kway Teow or equivalent Singapore hawker noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of Char Kway Teow weighs approximately 350 g, providing around 539 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Char Kway Teow — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05060005
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo Food Code F05060005 (Char Kway Teow) used as primary source. Values are directly referenced from the HPB database. Minor micronutrient fields (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimates where HPB data was unavailable, as documented in per_100g_meta.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — flat rice noodles (cooked)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 50 referenced for cooked flat rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database. Char Kway Teow is primarily composed of flat rice noodles; the GI of the composite dish is estimated using the rice noodle component as the dominant carbohydrate source. Fat, protein, and fibre from lard, egg, and vegetables provide modest glycaemic moderation. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (50 × 59.5) / 100 ≈ 29.8 for a 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 1848 mg per 350 g serving, representing ~80% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit, contributed by dark soy sauce, Chinese sausage, and cockle seasoning.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should consume Char Kway Teow only occasionally, request reduced soy sauce, and avoid the lard-based version to limit both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic index (~50) for flat rice noodles; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 59.5 g of carbohydrate, which is a substantial single-meal carbohydrate load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor post-meal blood glucose, as the high carbohydrate load per serving may cause significant glycaemic excursion despite the moderate GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from pork lard and Chinese sausage (2.5 g per 100 g; ~8.75 g per 350 g serving), combined with high sodium and dietary cholesterol (78 mg per 100 g; ~273 mg per serving).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid the traditional lard-based preparation, request a vegetable-oil version with reduced soy sauce, and limit consumption to occasional indulgence given the combined saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char Kway Teow (炒粿条) is one of Singapore's most iconic hawker dishes, originating among Hokkien and Teochew immigrants and now a staple at hawker centres island-wide. The dish consists of flat rice noodles (kway teow) — and sometimes yellow wheat noodles — stir-fried at extremely high heat in a screaming-hot wok to achieve 'wok hei', the characteristic smoky, caramelised aroma. Traditional preparation uses pork lard and crunchy lard bits as the cooking fat, imparting a rich, silky texture. Key ingredients include fresh blood cockles (hum), Chinese sausage (lap cheong), fishcake slices, bean sprouts, garlic chives, and egg, seasoned with dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, and chilli. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 154 kcal, 6.8 g protein, 7.2 g fat (2.5 g saturated), and 17 g carbohydrate. A standard 350 g hawker plate delivers ~539 kcal, ~59.5 g carbohydrate, and ~1848 mg sodium. The dish is dairy-free but is not halal due to pork lard and Chinese sausage. It is high in sodium and saturated fat, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The moderate glycaemic index (~50) of the rice noodle base means blood glucose impact is partially moderated by fat and protein, but the high carbohydrate load per serving remains significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (Food Code F05060005), an approved primary source for Singapore dishes. Three minor micronutrient fields (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimates at confidence 0.9. GI sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database using the flat rice noodle analogue, which is the dominant carbohydrate component of the dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite hawker dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite stir-fried dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of April 2026. copper_mg (0.12), manganese_mg (0.25), and vitamin_k_mcg (12.0) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimates (confidence 0.9) as these micronutrients are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo entry. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on cooked flat rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for the composite Char Kway Teow dish has been published. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as (50 × 59.5) / 100 = 29.8. The dish is not halal due to pork lard and Chinese sausage (lap cheong). Portions in the input ranged from 350 g to 450 g across sources; 350 g is used as the standard serving size based on the HPB-verified source. Duplicate portion entries (two × 380 g from enriched_json) have been consolidated.
